main_summary.summary: Juadine Henderson, interviewed December 10, 2015 in Washington by Emilye Crosby for the Civil Rights History Project, recounts her movement work from Panola County, Mississippi through SNCC, the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party, the Mississippi Freedom Labor Union, the Delta Ministry, SCLC's Poor People's Campaign, and the Drum and Spear Bookstore in Washington. Born in Batesville to a landowning family in Section 7, she encountered the movement at age thirteen when SNCC organizer Frank Smith visited at Thanksgiving 1962. She attended a SNCC voter-registration workshop in Greenwood in June 1963, learning of Medgar Evers's assassination on arrival. Returning home, she helped organize meetings, sit-ins at Batesville restaurants, and was arrested as a juvenile in 1964. After graduating high school in 1965, she joined the Delta Ministry's Freedom Corps with Curtis Hayes in Rosedale, organizing farm workers earning thirty cents an hour. She was jailed in Jackson during a 1965 demonstration and held two weeks at the Fairgrounds, and overnight at Parchman after a Rosedale arrest. She attended Bishop College in Texas (1966-1968), then organized for the 1968 Poor People's Campaign before settling in Washington to work at the Institute for Policy Studies, Drum and Spear Bookstore, the Black Child Development Institute, and eventually completing the Michele Clark Fellowship at Columbia in 1974 to become a journalist for twenty-eight years.
key_themes: Mississippi Freedom Labor Union and Delta Ministry
Armed self-defense alongside nonviolent direct action
main_summary.historical_significance:
Henderson's testimony documents the SNCC organizing trajectory from rural northern Mississippi (Batesville/Panola County) through the Delta plantations, distinct from the better-documented Greenwood and McComb work. Her account of the Mississippi Freedom Labor Union, the Delta Ministry's Freedom Corps stipend program funded by the National Council of Churches, the Strike City tent encampment, and the Greenville Air Force Base occupation fills out the 1965-1966 economic-rights organizing layer of the Mississippi Movement.

The interview also preserves first-person detail on the Drum and Spear Bookstore in Washington, the Institute for Policy Studies fellows program, the Sauti ya Watoto radio program, and the Center for Black Education — the post-1968 Black cultural-institution network that movement veterans built in DC. Henderson's recollection of the Panola County drum-and-fife tradition (Othar Turner, Sid Hemphill) traced by the Smithsonian to West African Mali rhythms documents an unrecognized African retention within her own neighborhood.

chapters.title: Batesville Childhood and Section 7
chapters.summary: Henderson recounts her birth in Batesville, Mississippi, her family's small farm in Section 7 of Panola County, her father's death when she was four or five, and her mother's remarriage. She describes her grandmother's church-affiliated one-room school where she began at age three, working in Brown's Cafe with her mother, and the segregation she first noticed at the restaurant.

chapters.title: Early Awareness and West Camp Church
chapters.summary: Henderson describes learning about the NAACP through her mother's secret membership card, the Emmett Till coverage in Jet magazine, and Reverend Middleton Godfrey's Black history sermons at West Camp Missionary Baptist Church. She recounts the Kirkendall family incident where the sheriff demanded a father beat his daughter, the community's armed protective response, and the family's flight to Indiana.

chapters.title: Frank Smith and the Greenwood Workshop
chapters.summary: Henderson recalls meeting SNCC organizer Frank Smith at her cousin's house at Thanksgiving 1962, traveling to a Greenwood voter-registration workshop in June 1963 with her cousin Jimmy and John Hardy, arriving the morning Medgar Evers was assassinated, meeting John O'Neal, Willie Peacock, and the Peacock brothers, and learning about Lawrence Guyot, Fannie Lou Hamer, June Johnson, and Euvester Simpson's disappearance and beating.
related_events: Winona beating of Hamer, Johnson, Simpson

chapters.title: Batesville Movement and Sit-ins
chapters.summary: Henderson describes returning to Batesville to organize, her mother and stepfather registering to vote in 1964, the 1964 Mississippi Summer Project arrival, restaurant sit-ins with white farmers attacking demonstrators, her juvenile-court arrests, and Robert Miles's protective intervention. She recounts the older generation's armed self-defense — women carrying pistols to West Camp meetings — alongside the nonviolent movement work.

chapters.title: Mount Beulah and the Jackson Fairgrounds
chapters.summary: Henderson recounts her decision after high-school graduation to delay college and join the movement, hitching a ride to Jackson with Lewis 'Lou' Grant, staying at Mount Beulah with Batesville women, being arrested at a Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party demonstration, and spending two weeks at the Jackson Fairgrounds with roughly a thousand women. She describes Mrs. Thelma Glover's prayer that defused a police standoff over a mattress.

chapters.title: Freedom Corps and the Mississippi Freedom Labor Union
chapters.summary: Henderson describes joining Curtis Hayes in McComb, then Cleveland, Mississippi as part of the Delta Ministry's Freedom Corps stipended by the National Council of Churches. She lived with Owen Brooks and the Washington family, then moved to Rosedale to organize with Frank Davis, Billy Kochiyama, Morton Thomas, Isaac Foster, and Homer Crawford for the Mississippi Freedom Labor Union among Delta plantation workers earning thirty cents an hour.

chapters.title: Bishop College and Federal Prison Visits
chapters.summary: Henderson describes leaving Mississippi for Bishop College in Texas in 1966 with Marjorie Hyatt and Linda Wilson, the campus reaction to her afro, working in the laundry and then the library, and her Sunday visits to Lafayette Loche at Seagoville federal prison with a Louisiana woman whose fiance was a war resister. She recounts the Parchman state-prison overnight detention after a Rosedale arrest, where women were given a forced laxative drink.

chapters.title: Poor People's Campaign and Resurrection City
chapters.summary: Henderson recounts Dr. King's 1968 Batesville visit, her work with Leon Hall organizing the Mule Train for SCLC's Poor People's Campaign from Memphis through Selma and Montgomery to Birmingham and Washington. She describes the contrast between Resurrection City tents on the National Mall and SCLC staff housing at the Pitts Motor Inn, meeting Charles E. Cobb Jr. on 14th Street, and being extracted by Frank Smith to the Institute for Policy Studies.

chapters.title: Drum and Spear Bookstore
chapters.summary: Henderson describes her job at Drum and Spear Bookstore in Washington — founded through Afro-American Resources — working with Charles E. Cobb Jr., Tony Gittens, and Ralph Featherstone, the connection to the Center for Black Education and Federal City College, the Sauti ya Watoto radio program with Mimi (Curtis Hayes's wife), the tear-gassing during Lerone Bennett Jr's book signing, and Featherstone's death by car bomb with Che Payne on March 10, 1970.

chapters.title: Panola County Drums and Family History
chapters.summary: Henderson describes the Panola County drum-and-fife tradition played every July 3-4 by Othar Turner and earlier Sid Hemphill, traced by Smithsonian recordings to West African Mali rhythms. She recounts her family history: her great-great-grandfather Duncan C. Henderson as slave owner, the 1952 family incident in Pope involving her uncles, and her uncle's electrocution. She also recounts Benjamin Brown's killing at Jackson State.

chapters.title: Journalism Career and Reflections
chapters.summary: Henderson recounts her path through Children's Defense Fund, the Interreligious Foundation for Community Organization, the National Urban Coalition, the Michele Clark Fellowship at Columbia in 1974 working with Earl Caldwell, her first job at the Daily Argus in Mount Vernon, and twenty-eight years as a journalist. She reflects on the 1966 Meredith March, the SNCC Nashville meeting electing Stokely Carmichael, the Deacons for Defense, and her concerns about the current political climate.
